sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS manual directly supports the 484110 definition, palletized/container-or-van characterization, metropolitan-area scope, same-day-return norm, and relevant cross-references. It provides domain context rather than direct measurements of eligibility or operator-required share, consistent with the record's ESTIMATE/PROXY treatment.

The May 2023 NAICS 484000 OEWS table verifies total employment of 1,577,900, driver/sales workers and truck drivers at 62.99%, transportation and material moving occupations at 74.75%, office and administrative support at 11.32%, dispatchers at 2.42%, and the published wage fields.

The December 2024 TTAC report supports freight uses including multi-stop route optimization, predictive maintenance, shipment inquiry and update chatbots, and invoice and bill-of-lading processing, as well as hallucination/context, privacy, cybersecurity, and data-quality risks.

The February 2025 BLS article says autonomous-trucking effects were too uncertain for short-to-medium-term model adjustment, cites regulatory and public-safety constraints, and reports no meaningful employment impact through 2023.

Gallup verifies the fall-2024 survey population and reports a 22% five-year sell-or-transfer plan rate among employer-business owners; it separately attributes the 52.3% age-55-or-older employer-business statistic to Census data. The source measures intentions, not completed qualifying transfers, as disclosed.

The BTS/FHWA release verifies projected U.S. freight-tonnage growth of 50% from 2020 to 2050, current truck carriage of 65% of tonnage, and trucks' expected continued predominance. Its national, multimodal, long-horizon population is disclosed in the bridge.

The BTS indicators page states that spot-market trucking loads are approximately one-tenth of the overall common-carrier trucking market and defines those loads as shipments outside a pre-existing hauling contract.

The 2004 DOT-associated research record and abstract characterize trucking as highly competitive, easy to enter, relatively undifferentiated, and slim-margin, and discuss routing and dispatch under uncertain information. Its age and broad industry scope are disclosed.

The current BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook page projects 4% growth in heavy and tractor-trailer truck-driver employment from 2024 to 2034 and verifies physical, inspection, cargo-securement, logging, regulatory, routing, and maintenance-reporting duties.

GAO-19-161 verifies that development centered on long-haul operations and highway portions of routes, that widespread deployment was viewed as years or decades away, and that one stakeholder scenario retained operators for complex driving and non-driving tasks.

caveats: The a base of 0.17 depends on an analyst-assigned task-exposure map applied to four-digit NAICS 484 occupation and wage data; the packet does not show the underlying occupation-by-occupation weights. The PROXY label, bridge, and wide range make this usable, and rho already leaves H and the action estimate-conditioned.

The e base of 0.82 is not empirically measured: S1 establishes the industry boundary but not recurring revenue, owner independence, compliance quality, customer concentration, or transferability. This is honestly labeled ESTIMATE and does not create a material review finding under the specified tier/action test.

The s5 base of 0.18 converts cross-industry stated intentions into completed qualifying transfers without observed realization data; Gallup's surveyed employer owners are also much broader than the target EBITDA band. The discount and uncertainty range are explicit, and the record remains MODEL_CONDITIONED.

The q base of 0.40 is a judgment rather than a measured pass-through rate. S7 is overall common-carrier trucking and S8 is a broad 2004 industry study, so neither directly establishes five-year savings retention for local LMM carriers; the ESTIMATE label and 0.20-0.62 range are appropriate.

The d5 and o bridges combine national multimodal long-horizon freight projections with occupation evidence weighted toward heavy and long-haul driving. Those sources support direction and physical-task persistence, not a direct five-year local-service quantity or operator-required share; the record discloses these proxy limitations and spans a meaningful downside case.
summary: Mechanics pass and all ten primitive-cited sources were reachable and substantively support the raw claims attributed to them. The default lens is coherent, evidence states are honest, construct boundaries are respected, ranges expose meaningful uncertainty, and the LOW_PRIORITY conclusion follows from the weak labor-opportunity factor. The record is publishable with caveats because several central values remain broad, disclosed judgments or cross-population proxies, but no correction or honest downgrade identified in this review would change the base tier or the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
